lessons.title: Docker Fundamentals
objectives: Understand container internals: namespaces, cgroups, and overlay filesystems
Write efficient, production-quality Dockerfiles with all major instructions
Build optimized ML/HPC images using multi-stage builds
Manage multi-service stacks with Docker Compose
Apply security best practices: non-root users, .dockerignore, image scanning
lessons.content_md:
# Docker Fundamentals

## 1. What Is a Container?

A container is **not** a lightweight VM. It is a regular Linux process
whose view of the system is restricted by three kernel features:

| Kernel Feature       | Purpose                                       |
|----------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| **Namespaces**       | Isolate what a process can *see*               |
| **Cgroups**          | Limit what a process can *use*                 |
| **Overlay FS**       | Provide a layered, copy-on-write filesystem    |

### 1.1 Linux Namespaces

Namespaces partition kernel resources so each set of processes has its
own isolated view. Docker uses six namespaces:

| Namespace | Flag            | Isolates                          |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------------------------|
| PID       | `CLONE_NEWPID`  | Process IDs вҖ” PID 1 inside        |
| NET       | `CLONE_NEWNET`  | Network interfaces, routes, ports |
| MNT       | `CLONE_NEWNS`   | Mount points (filesystem tree)    |
| UTS       | `CLONE_NEWUTS`  | Hostname and domain name          |
| IPC       | `CLONE_NEWIPC`  | Shared memory, semaphores         |
| USER      | `CLONE_NEWUSER` | UID/GID mappings                  |

You can verify namespace isolation from the host:

```bash
# Run a container and inspect its namespaces
docker run -d --name demo alpine sleep 3600
PID=$(docker inspect --format '{{.State.Pid}}' demo)
ls -la /proc/$PID/ns/
# Each entry is a symlink to a unique namespace inode
```

### 1.2 Control Groups (cgroups)

Cgroups enforce resource limits on CPU, memory, I/O, and devices.
When you run `docker run --memory=4g --cpus=2`, Docker creates a
cgroup with those constraints:

```bash
# Inside the container
cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory/memory.limit_in_bytes   # cgroup v1
cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory.max                      # cgroup v2
```

For GPU containers, the NVIDIA runtime adds device cgroup rules that
whitelist `/dev/nvidia*` device nodes.

### 1.3 Overlay Filesystem and Image Layers

Docker images use a **union filesystem** (OverlayFS) that stacks
read-only layers on top of each other, with a thin writable layer
added when a container starts:

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ   Container layer (R/W)         в”Ӯ  вҶҗ writable; lost when removed
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ Layer 5: CMD ["python","app.py"]в”Ӯ  вҶҗ 0 B   (metadata only)
в”Ӯ Layer 4: COPY . /app            в”Ӯ  вҶҗ 15 MB  (app source code)
в”Ӯ Layer 3: RUN pip install -r ... в”Ӯ  вҶҗ 350 MB (Python packages)
в”Ӯ Layer 2: RUN apt-get install ...в”Ӯ  вҶҗ 120 MB (system packages)
в”Ӯ Layer 1: FROM ubuntu:22.04      в”Ӯ  вҶҗ 77 MB  (base OS)
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
            Read-Only Layers
```

Each layer stores only the **diff** from the layer below. This
enables:
- **Sharing**: multiple images sharing the same base layer save disk
- **Caching**: unchanged layers are reused during builds
- **Fast startup**: no full filesystem copy required

:::interactive{component="DockerLayerVisualizer"}

## 2. Dockerfile Deep Dive

A Dockerfile is a declarative recipe for building images. Every
instruction creates a layer (or metadata entry).

### 2.1 Core Instructions Reference

```dockerfile
# Base image вҖ” always start here
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.2.0-devel-ubuntu22.04 AS builder

# Set environment variables (available at build AND runtime)
ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive \
    PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
    CUDA_HOME=/usr/local/cuda

# Set the working directory
WORKDIR /app

# Install system packages вҖ” combine to reduce layers
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
        build-essential \
        cmake \
        git \
        libopenmpi-dev \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Copy dependency manifest first (cache-friendly ordering)
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# COPY vs ADD:
#   COPY вҖ” simple file/directory copy (preferred)
#   ADD  вҖ” also handles URLs and tar auto-extraction
COPY src/ ./src/
COPY configs/ ./configs/

# Expose a port (documentation; does not publish)
EXPOSE 8080

# CMD вҖ” default command (can be overridden at runtime)
CMD ["python", "src/serve.py"]

# ENTRYPOINT вҖ” fixed executable (CMD becomes arguments)
# ENTRYPOINT ["python", "src/serve.py"]
```

### 2.2 ENTRYPOINT vs CMD

| Dockerfile             | `docker run img`       | `docker run img bash`    |
|------------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| `CMD ["python"]`       | `python`               | `bash`                   |
| `ENTRYPOINT ["python"]`| `python`               | `python bash`            |
| Both combined          | `python app.py`        | `python bash`            |

Best practice: use `ENTRYPOINT` for the executable and `CMD` for
default arguments:

```dockerfile
ENTRYPOINT ["torchrun", "--nproc_per_node=auto"]
CMD ["train.py", "--epochs=10"]
```

:::interactive{component="TerminalSimulator" config='{"preset":"docker-basics"}'}

## 3. Multi-Stage Builds

ML/HPC images often balloon to 10+ GB because build tools, headers,
and source code remain in the final image. Multi-stage builds solve
this by using separate stages for building and running:

```dockerfile
# ---- Stage 1: Builder ----
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.2.0-devel-ubuntu22.04 AS builder

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
        build-essential cmake git && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

WORKDIR /build
COPY . .
RUN mkdir -p output && cmake -B output -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release . \
    && cmake --build output --parallel $(nproc)

# ---- Stage 2: Runtime ----
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.2.0-runtime-ubuntu22.04

# Copy ONLY the compiled binary from the builder
COPY --from=builder /build/output/gpu_solver /usr/local/bin/gpu_solver

# Create a non-root user
RUN useradd --create-home appuser
USER appuser

ENTRYPOINT ["gpu_solver"]
```

| Metric       | Single stage | Multi-stage |
|--------------|-------------|-------------|
| Image size   | 8.2 GB      | 1.8 GB      |
| Attack surface | Large     | Minimal     |
| Build tools  | Included    | Excluded    |

:::interactive{component="DockerLayerVisualizer"}

## 4. Building ML/HPC Images

### 4.1 CUDA Base Images

NVIDIA provides official base images on NGC:

| Tag Pattern                          | Contents                         |
|--------------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| `nvidia/cuda:12.2.0-base-ubuntu22.04`   | Driver libs only (~120 MB)   |
| `nvidia/cuda:12.2.0-runtime-ubuntu22.04` | + CUDA runtime (~1.5 GB)    |
| `nvidia/cuda:12.2.0-devel-ubuntu22.04`   | + headers, nvcc (~3.5 GB)   |

Rule of thumb: use `devel` for building, `runtime` for deployment.

### 4.2 Conda Environments in Docker

```dockerfile
FROM continuumio/miniconda3:24.1.2-0

COPY environment.yml .
RUN conda env create -f environment.yml && conda clean -afy
SHELL ["conda", "run", "-n", "myenv", "/bin/bash", "-c"]

COPY src/ ./src/
ENTRYPOINT ["conda", "run", "--no-capture-output", "-n", "myenv", \
             "python", "src/train.py"]
```

### 4.3 MPI in Docker

```dockerfile
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.2.0-devel-ubuntu22.04

# Install OpenMPI with CUDA-awareness
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
        libopenmpi-dev openmpi-bin openssh-client && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

ENV OMPI_MCA_btl_tcp_if_include=eth0
ENV OMPI_ALLOW_RUN_AS_ROOT=1
ENV OMPI_ALLOW_RUN_AS_ROOT_CONFIRM=1

COPY . /app
WORKDIR /app
RUN make mpi_solver

ENTRYPOINT ["mpirun"]
CMD ["--bind-to", "none", "-np", "4", "./mpi_solver"]
```

## 5. Docker Compose for Multi-Service Stacks

Real ML workflows involve multiple services вҖ” a training API, an
experiment tracker, a database, a monitoring stack:

```yaml
version: "3.9"
services:
  jupyter:
    image: quay.io/jupyter/pytorch-notebook:cuda12-latest
    ports: ["8888:8888"]
    volumes:
      - ./notebooks:/home/jovyan/work
      - training-data:/data:ro
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: 1
              capabilities: [gpu]

  mlflow:
    image: ghcr.io/mlflow/mlflow:v2.14.0
    ports: ["5000:5000"]
    environment:
      BACKEND_STORE_URI: postgresql://mlflow:secret@postgres:5432/mlflow
      DEFAULT_ARTIFACT_ROOT: /mlflow-artifacts
    volumes:
      - mlflow-artifacts:/mlflow-artifacts
    depends_on: [postgres]

  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: mlflow
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secret
      POSTGRES_DB: mlflow
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data

volumes:
  training-data:
  mlflow-artifacts:
  pgdata:
```

Start the stack:

```bash
docker compose up -d          # Start all services
docker compose ps              # Check status
docker compose logs -f mlflow  # Stream logs
docker compose down -v         # Stop and remove volumes
```

## 6. Best Practices

### 6.1 Minimize Layers and Image Size

```dockerfile
# BAD вҖ” three layers, apt cache left behind
RUN apt-get update
RUN apt-get install -y python3
RUN rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# GOOD вҖ” one layer, apt cache cleaned in same layer
RUN apt-get update && \
    apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends python3 && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
```

### 6.2 .dockerignore

Prevent build context bloat (large datasets, .git, virtualenvs):

```
.git
__pycache__
*.pyc
.venv
data/
*.tar.gz
checkpoints/
wandb/
.env
```

### 6.3 Non-Root Users

```dockerfile
RUN groupadd -r appgroup && useradd -r -g appgroup -m appuser
USER appuser
WORKDIR /home/appuser/app
```

### 6.4 Security Scanning

```bash
# Scan with Trivy
trivy image myregistry/ml-api:latest

# Scan with Docker Scout
docker scout cves myregistry/ml-api:latest
```

### 6.5 Build Cache Best Practices

Order your Dockerfile from least-changing to most-changing:

1. `FROM` (base image вҖ” rarely changes)
2. System packages (`apt-get` вҖ” changes occasionally)
3. Dependency manifest (`requirements.txt` вҖ” changes sometimes)
4. Application source (`COPY . .` вҖ” changes every build)

exercises.title: Write a PyTorch CUDA Dockerfile
exercises.description:
Write a Dockerfile that builds a PyTorch training environment. Start from
the nvidia/cuda:12.2.0-runtime-ubuntu22.04 base image, install Python 3.11,
pip install PyTorch with CUDA support and torchvision from requirements.txt,
copy the application source, create a non-root user 'trainer', and set the
entrypoint to run train.py.
exercises.starter_code:
# Dockerfile for PyTorch CUDA Training Environment
# TODO: Choose the correct NVIDIA CUDA base image

# TODO: Set environment variables to prevent interactive prompts
#       and enable unbuffered Python output

# TODO: Install Python 3.11 and pip (combine into a single RUN layer
#       and clean up apt cache)

# TODO: Set working directory to /app

# TODO: Copy requirements.txt first, then install dependencies
#       (for optimal layer caching)

# TODO: Copy the application source code

# TODO: Create a non-root user called 'trainer' and switch to it

# TODO: Set the entrypoint to run train.py with python
hints: Use FROM nvidia/cuda:12.2.0-runtime-ubuntu22.04 as the base.
Set DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive and PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1.
Always COPY requirements.txt before COPY . for cache efficiency.
Use useradd --create-home trainer to create the user.
exercises.solution:
```dockerfile
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.2.0-runtime-ubuntu22.04

ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
    PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
        python3.11 python3-pip python3.11-venv && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* && \
    ln -sf /usr/bin/python3.11 /usr/bin/python

WORKDIR /app

COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

COPY . .

RUN useradd --create-home trainer
USER trainer

ENTRYPOINT ["python", "train.py"]
```

exercises.title: Optimize Image Size with Multi-Stage Build
exercises.description:
The following Dockerfile produces a 9 GB image because it includes all
build tools in the final image. Rewrite it as a multi-stage build where
Stage 1 (builder) compiles the CUDA C++ application using the devel image,
and Stage 2 uses the runtime image with only the compiled binary. The source
is in /src and the Makefile produces binary 'gpu_app'.
exercises.starter_code:
# CURRENT (unoptimized) вҖ” produces ~9 GB image
# FROM nvidia/cuda:12.2.0-devel-ubuntu22.04
# RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y build-essential cmake
# COPY . /src
# WORKDIR /src
# RUN make gpu_app
# ENTRYPOINT ["./gpu_app"]

# TODO: Rewrite as a multi-stage build
# Stage 1: "builder" вҖ” use the devel image, install build-essential,
#   copy source, compile with make

# Stage 2: runtime вҖ” use the runtime image, copy ONLY the compiled
#   binary from the builder stage, set entrypoint
hints: Name the first stage with AS builder.
Use COPY --from=builder to pull the binary into the runtime stage.
The runtime stage should NOT have build-essential installed.
exercises.solution:
```dockerfile
# Stage 1: Build
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.2.0-devel-ubuntu22.04 AS builder
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
        build-essential cmake && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
COPY . /src
WORKDIR /src
RUN make -j$(nproc) gpu_app

# Stage 2: Runtime
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.2.0-runtime-ubuntu22.04
COPY --from=builder /src/gpu_app /usr/local/bin/gpu_app
RUN useradd --create-home appuser
USER appuser
ENTRYPOINT ["gpu_app"]
```
This reduces the image from ~9 GB to ~1.8 GB by excluding
compilers, headers, source code, and object files.

exercises.title: Docker Compose GPU ML Stack
exercises.description:
Write a docker-compose.yaml that defines three services: (1) a Jupyter
notebook server with GPU access using quay.io/jupyter/pytorch-notebook:cuda12-latest,
exposing port 8888 and mounting ./notebooks; (2) an MLflow tracking server
using ghcr.io/mlflow/mlflow:v2.14.0 on port 5000; (3) a Redis cache on port
6379. All services should be on a custom network called 'ml-net'. Jupyter
should reserve 1 GPU.
exercises.starter_code:
# docker-compose.yaml for ML Development Stack
version: "3.9"

services:
  # TODO: Define Jupyter service
  #   - image: quay.io/jupyter/pytorch-notebook:cuda12-latest
  #   - port 8888
  #   - mount ./notebooks to /home/jovyan/work
  #   - reserve 1 GPU using deploy.resources.reservations.devices
  #   - connect to ml-net network

  # TODO: Define MLflow service
  #   - image: ghcr.io/mlflow/mlflow:v2.14.0
  #   - port 5000
  #   - connect to ml-net network

  # TODO: Define Redis service
  #   - image: redis:7-alpine
  #   - port 6379
  #   - connect to ml-net network

# TODO: Define the custom network 'ml-net'
networks:
hints: GPU access requires deploy.resources.reservations.devices with driver: nvidia.
Use a bind mount (./notebooks:/home/jovyan/work) for the notebooks.
Define ml-net under the top-level 'networks' key with driver: bridge.
exercises.solution:
```yaml
version: "3.9"
services:
  jupyter:
    image: quay.io/jupyter/pytorch-notebook:cuda12-latest
    ports:
      - "8888:8888"
    volumes:
      - ./notebooks:/home/jovyan/work
    networks:
      - ml-net
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: 1
              capabilities: [gpu]

  mlflow:
    image: ghcr.io/mlflow/mlflow:v2.14.0
    ports:
      - "5000:5000"
    networks:
      - ml-net

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    networks:
      - ml-net

networks:
  ml-net:
    driver: bridge
```

Write a comprehensive .dockerignore file for an ML project that prevents
large datasets, model checkpoints, Python cache files, virtual environments,
Git history, Weights & Biases logs, and environment secrets from being
included in the Docker build context.

# TODO: Exclude environment/secret files

# TODO: Exclude IDE configuration
hints: Include .git, __pycache__, *.pyc, .venv, data/, checkpoints/.
Don't forget .env files which may contain secrets.
Exclude wandb/ and mlruns/ experiment tracking directories.

```
.gitignore

*.pyc

env/
```

exercises.title: Conda Environment Dockerfile
exercises.description:
Write a Dockerfile that creates a reproducible ML environment using
Miniconda. Start from continuumio/miniconda3:24.1.2-0, copy an
environment.yml file, create the conda environment named 'hpc-ml',
clean the conda cache, and set the entrypoint to run scripts within
that environment using 'conda run'.
exercises.starter_code:
# Dockerfile for Conda-based ML Environment
# TODO: Start from the miniconda3 base image

# TODO: Set working directory

# TODO: Copy conda environment file and create the environment
#       (clean conda cache to reduce image size)

# TODO: Set the default shell to run within the conda environment

# TODO: Copy application source

# TODO: Set entrypoint using conda run
hints: Use 'conda env create -f environment.yml' to create from the YAML.
Run 'conda clean -afy' to remove downloaded packages and caches.
The ENTRYPOINT should use conda run with --no-capture-output.
exercises.solution:
```dockerfile
FROM continuumio/miniconda3:24.1.2-0

WORKDIR /app

COPY environment.yml .
RUN conda env create -f environment.yml && \
    conda clean -afy

SHELL ["conda", "run", "-n", "hpc-ml", "/bin/bash", "-c"]

COPY . .

ENTRYPOINT ["conda", "run", "--no-capture-output", "-n", "hpc-ml", \
             "python"]
CMD ["train.py"]
```

lessons.title: Container Runtimes for HPC вҖ” Singularity/Apptainer
objectives: Explain why Docker is unsuitable for shared HPC clusters and how Singularity solves this
Understand the SIF image format and how it differs from Docker layers
Write definition files (.def) and convert Docker images to SIF
Run containers with exec, run, shell, and instance commands
Configure bind mounts, GPU passthrough, and MPI integration
lessons.content_md:
# Container Runtimes for HPC вҖ” Singularity / Apptainer

## 1. Why Singularity for HPC?

Docker was designed for microservices: it runs a privileged daemon
as root, isolates containers in separate network namespaces, and
manages its own storage backend. **None of these properties are
acceptable on a shared HPC cluster:**

| Concern                | Docker                     | Apptainer                    |
|------------------------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| Privilege model        | Root daemon required       | Runs as calling user         |
| Privilege escalation   | Possible (docker group)    | Impossible by design         |
| Network model          | Isolated per container     | Shares host network          |
| Filesystem integration | Volume mounts only         | Auto-binds $HOME, /tmp, etc. |
| MPI support            | Complex, needs host net    | Native hybrid model          |
| GPU access             | `--gpus` flag + runtime    | `--nv` / `--rocm` flag       |
| Image format           | Layered OCI (requires daemon) | Single SIF file           |
| Scheduler integration  | Requires wrapper scripts   | Direct `srun apptainer ...`  |
| Reproducibility        | Mutable layers             | Immutable, cryptographically verifiable |

The critical insight: on an HPC cluster, an unprivileged user should
be able to pull or build a container and run it under their own UID
without any system service or elevated permissions.

### 1.1 The Security Model

When you run `apptainer exec container.sif command`:
- The process runs as **your UID/GID** on the host
- You have the **same permissions** inside the container as outside
- You **cannot** become root inside the container (unless you built
  with `--fakeroot`)
- The SIF file is **read-only** вҖ” you cannot modify the image at runtime

This means cluster admins can allow Apptainer without any risk of
users gaining elevated access.

## 2. SIF Format vs Docker Layers

| Aspect           | Docker OCI Image               | Apptainer SIF              |
|------------------|---------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Structure        | Set of tar.gz layer archives    | Single SquashFS file       |
| Mutability       | Layers can be modified          | Immutable after build      |
| Portability      | Requires registry or daemon     | Copy/scp the .sif file     |
| Verification     | Content-addressable digests     | Cryptographic signatures (PGP) |
| Size on disk     | Sum of all layers               | Compressed single file     |
| Startup overhead | Unpack layers to overlay        | Direct mount (near-zero)   |

A `.sif` file is a **Singularity Image Format** container вҖ” a
SquashFS filesystem wrapped with metadata. You can:

```bash
# Inspect the image
apptainer inspect container.sif
apptainer inspect --deffile container.sif   # View the definition

# Verify a signed image
apptainer verify container.sif

# List files in the image
apptainer exec container.sif ls /usr/local/bin/
```

:::interactive{component="DockerLayerVisualizer"}

## 3. Building Images

### 3.1 From Definition Files (.def)

A definition file is the Singularity equivalent of a Dockerfile:

```singularity
Bootstrap: docker
From: nvcr.io/nvidia/pytorch:24.01-py3

%labels
    Author HPC-AI Team
    Version 1.0
    Description PyTorch HPC training environment

%post
    # Runs as root during build
    apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
        ibverbs-utils \
        libibverbs-dev \
        numactl \
        && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

    pip install --no-cache-dir \
        transformers==4.40.0 \
        datasets==2.19.0 \
        accelerate==0.30.0 \
        deepspeed==0.14.0

%environment
    # Set at runtime
    export OMP_NUM_THREADS=1
    export NCCL_DEBUG=INFO
    export CUDA_DEVICE_ORDER=PCI_BUS_ID

%runscript
    # Executed when 'apptainer run container.sif' is called
    echo "PyTorch $(python -c 'import torch; print(torch.__version__)')"
    echo "CUDA available: $(python -c 'import torch; print(torch.cuda.is_available())')"
    exec python "$@"

%test
    # Run during 'apptainer build --run-tests'
    python -c "import torch; assert torch.cuda.is_available()"

%help
    PyTorch HPC training container with NCCL, DeepSpeed, and
    InfiniBand support. Usage:
        apptainer run --nv container.sif train.py --epochs 10
```

Build it:

```bash
# Build with fakeroot (no actual root required)
apptainer build --fakeroot pytorch-hpc.sif pytorch-hpc.def

# Build from a remote definition (sandbox for debugging)
apptainer build --sandbox pytorch-sandbox/ pytorch-hpc.def
apptainer shell --writable pytorch-sandbox/   # Interactive debugging
apptainer build pytorch-hpc.sif pytorch-sandbox/  # Finalize
```

### 3.2 Converting from Docker

```bash
# Pull directly from Docker Hub or NGC
apptainer pull pytorch.sif docker://nvcr.io/nvidia/pytorch:24.01-py3

# Pull from a private registry
apptainer pull --docker-login private.sif docker://myregistry.io/ml/train:v2

# Convert a local Docker image
docker save my-local-image:latest -o image.tar
apptainer build converted.sif docker-archive://image.tar
```

### 3.3 Multi-Stage Equivalent

Singularity doesn't have native multi-stage builds, but you can
chain builds:

```bash
# Stage 1: build in a sandbox
apptainer build --sandbox build-env/ builder.def
apptainer exec build-env/ make -C /src gpu_solver

# Stage 2: copy binary into minimal runtime
apptainer build runtime.sif runtime.def  # runtime.def uses %files from host
```

## 4. Running Containers

### 4.1 Core Run Modes

```bash
# exec вҖ” run a specific command
apptainer exec container.sif python train.py --epochs 10

# run вҖ” execute the %runscript
apptainer run container.sif --epochs 10

# shell вҖ” interactive shell inside the container
apptainer shell container.sif

# instance вҖ” start a background service
apptainer instance start container.sif myservice
apptainer exec instance://myservice curl http://localhost:8080
apptainer instance stop myservice
```

:::interactive{component="TerminalSimulator" config='{"preset":"singularity-basics"}'}

### 4.2 Environment Variables

```bash
# Pass env vars with --env
apptainer exec --env BATCH_SIZE=64 --env LR=0.001 \
    container.sif python train.py

# Pass a file of env vars
apptainer exec --env-file ./training.env container.sif python train.py

# Container's %environment vars are always set
# Host env vars are inherited by default (use --cleanenv to prevent)
apptainer exec --cleanenv --env CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0,1 \
    container.sif nvidia-smi
```

## 5. Bind Mounts for HPC Filesystems

By default, Apptainer automatically binds `$HOME`, `/tmp`, `/proc`,
`/sys`, and `/dev`. For HPC data directories, you need explicit binds:

```bash
apptainer exec \
    --bind /scratch/datasets:/data:ro \
    --bind /scratch/$USER/results:/results \
    --bind /lustre/shared/models:/models:ro \
    --bind /tmp/$SLURM_JOB_ID:/tmp \
    pytorch-hpc.sif python train.py \
        --data-dir /data \
        --model-dir /models \
        --output-dir /results
```

**Mount syntax:** `host_path:container_path[:ro]`

| Flag       | Effect                                           |
|------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| `:ro`      | Read-only mount (protects shared datasets)       |
| (default)  | Read-write mount                                 |
| `--no-home`| Do not mount $HOME (for reproducibility)         |

### 5.1 Overlay for Writable Layers

If you need to write to paths inside the container image:

```bash
# Create a writable overlay
apptainer overlay create --size 1024 overlay.img  # 1 GB

apptainer exec --overlay overlay.img container.sif \
    pip install new-package  # Writes go to overlay
```

## 6. GPU Support

### 6.1 NVIDIA GPUs (--nv)

```bash
apptainer exec --nv pytorch-hpc.sif python -c "
import torch
print(f'GPU count: {torch.cuda.device_count()}')
for i in range(torch.cuda.device_count()):
    print(f'  GPU {i}: {torch.cuda.get_device_name(i)}')
    print(f'    Memory: {torch.cuda.get_device_properties(i).total_mem / 1e9:.1f} GB')
"
```

The `--nv` flag does three things:
1. Bind-mounts `/dev/nvidia*` device files
2. Bind-mounts host NVIDIA driver libraries (`libcuda.so`, etc.)
3. Sets `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` to find the driver libraries

**Important**: The CUDA toolkit version in your container must be
compatible with the host driver version. Use `nvidia-smi` on the
host to check the maximum supported CUDA version.

### 6.2 AMD GPUs (--rocm)

```bash
apptainer exec --rocm pytorch-rocm.sif python -c "
import torch; print(torch.cuda.is_available())  # ROCm uses same API
"
```

## 7. MPI Integration

### 7.1 Hybrid Approach (Recommended)

The host MPI launcher invokes Apptainer, which calls the
application's MPI inside the container:

```bash
srun --mpi=pmix apptainer exec --nv \
    --bind /scratch/data:/data \
    --bind /opt/slurm:/opt/slurm \
    mpi-app.sif /app/solver --input /data/mesh.h5
```

Requirements:
- Host MPI and container MPI must be **ABI-compatible**
- Same major version of OpenMPI or MPICH
- PMI/PMIx libraries must match

### 7.2 Bind Approach

Bind-mount the host MPI into the container:

```bash
apptainer exec \
    --bind /opt/openmpi:/opt/openmpi \
    --bind /opt/slurm:/opt/slurm \
    container.sif /app/solver
```

This avoids version mismatch issues but couples the container to
the specific cluster's MPI installation.

### 7.3 Complete Multi-Node SLURM Job

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=distributed-train
#SBATCH --partition=gpu
#SBATCH --nodes=4
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:a100:8
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=64
#SBATCH --mem=512G
#SBATCH --time=48:00:00

module load apptainer

export MASTER_ADDR=$(scontrol show hostnames $SLURM_JOB_NODELIST | head -n1)
export MASTER_PORT=29500

srun apptainer exec --nv \
    --bind /scratch/datasets:/data:ro \
    --bind /scratch/$USER/checkpoints:/checkpoints \
    /shared/containers/pytorch-hpc.sif \
    torchrun \
        --nnodes=$SLURM_NNODES \
        --nproc_per_node=8 \
        --rdzv_id=$SLURM_JOB_ID \
        --rdzv_backend=c10d \
        --rdzv_endpoint=$MASTER_ADDR:$MASTER_PORT \
        train.py \
            --data /data \
            --output /checkpoints \
            --bf16 --batch-size 64
```

exercises.description:
Write an Apptainer definition file for an MPI-enabled HPC simulation.
Bootstrap from ubuntu:22.04, install OpenMPI with InfiniBand support
(libibverbs-dev, librdmacm-dev, openmpi-bin, libopenmpi-dev), compile
a C++ source file /src/mpi_solver.cpp, and set up the environment for
InfiniBand networking.
exercises.starter_code:
# mpi-solver.def вҖ” MPI HPC Container
# TODO: Bootstrap from ubuntu:22.04

# TODO: %post section
#   - Install OpenMPI with InfiniBand support packages
#   - Install build-essential, cmake
#   - Copy and compile the MPI application

# TODO: %environment section
#   - Set MPI and InfiniBand environment variables

# TODO: %runscript
#   - Execute the compiled solver
hints: InfiniBand requires libibverbs-dev, librdmacm-dev, ibverbs-utils.
Set OMPI_MCA_btl=^tcp to prefer InfiniBand over TCP.
Include %test to verify mpirun works.
exercises.solution:
```singularity
Bootstrap: docker
From: ubuntu:22.04

%labels
    Author HPC-AI Team
    Description MPI solver with InfiniBand support

%post
    apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
        build-essential cmake \
        openmpi-bin libopenmpi-dev \
        libibverbs-dev librdmacm-dev ibverbs-utils \
        && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

    mkdir -p /app
    cp /src/mpi_solver.cpp /app/
    cd /app && mpicxx -O3 -o mpi_solver mpi_solver.cpp

%environment
    export OMPI_MCA_btl=openib,self
    export OMPI_MCA_btl_tcp_if_exclude=lo,docker0
    export UCX_TLS=rc,ud,sm,self

%runscript
    exec /app/mpi_solver "$@"

%test
    mpirun --version
    /app/mpi_solver --help || true
```

# Kubernetes Core Concepts

## 1. Architecture Overview

Kubernetes (K8s) is a container orchestration platform that automates
deployment, scaling, and management of containerized workloads.

```
  в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ                    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Pod A в”Ӯ в”Ӯ Pod B в”Ӯ в”Ӯ Pod C в”Ӯ                    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                    в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

:::interactive{component="K8sVisualizer" config='{"preset":"web-app"}'}

### 1.1 Control Plane Components

| Component              | Role                                          |
|------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| **API Server**         | Central hub; all communication goes through it |
| **etcd**               | Distributed key-value store for cluster state  |
| **Scheduler**          | Assigns Pods to nodes based on resources/constraints |
| **Controller Manager** | Runs controllers that reconcile desired state  |

### 1.2 Worker Node Components

| Component              | Role                                         |
|------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| **kubelet**            | Agent on each node; manages Pod lifecycle    |
| **kube-proxy**         | Maintains network rules for Service routing  |
| **Container Runtime**  | Runs containers (containerd, CRI-O)          |

:::interactive{component="ProcessSchedulerViz"}

## 2. Pods

A **Pod** is the smallest deployable unit вҖ” one or more containers
that share the same network namespace, storage volumes, and lifecycle.

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: ml-inference
  namespace: ml-serving
  labels:
    app: inference
    team: ml-platform
spec:
  containers:
    - name: model-server
      image: nvcr.io/nvidia/tritonserver:24.01-py3
      ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
          name: http
        - containerPort: 8001
          name: grpc
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: "4"
          memory: "16Gi"
        limits:
          cpu: "8"
          memory: "32Gi"
          nvidia.com/gpu: 1
      volumeMounts:
        - name: model-repo
          mountPath: /models
          readOnly: true
      readinessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /v2/health/ready
          port: 8000
        initialDelaySeconds: 30
        periodSeconds: 10
      livenessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /v2/health/live
          port: 8000
        initialDelaySeconds: 60
        periodSeconds: 30
  volumes:
    - name: model-repo
      persistentVolumeClaim:
        claimName: model-store-pvc
  restartPolicy: Always
```

### 2.1 Resource Requests vs Limits

| Field      | Meaning                                              |
|------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| `requests` | Minimum guaranteed resources; used for **scheduling** |
| `limits`   | Maximum allowed; enforced by **cgroups** at runtime   |

```
requests вүӨ actual usage вүӨ limits

в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  Node capacity: 64 CPUs, 256 Gi memory     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Pod A requests: 4 CPU, 16 Gi  в”Җв”Җв”җ          в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Pod B requests: 8 CPU, 32 Gi  в”Җв”Җв”јв”Җ total   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Pod C requests: 4 CPU, 16 Gi  в”Җв”Җв”ҳ scheduled в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Remaining allocatable: 48 CPU, 192 Gi      в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

**Best practice for HPC/ML**: Set requests = limits for GPU workloads
to get **Guaranteed** QoS class, preventing eviction.

## 3. Deployments

A **Deployment** manages a ReplicaSet and provides declarative
updates with rollback support:

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: inference-api
  namespace: ml-serving
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: inference-api
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: inference-api
        version: v2.1.0
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: api
          image: myregistry/inference-api:v2.1.0
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "2"
              memory: "8Gi"
            limits:
              cpu: "4"
              memory: "16Gi"
              nvidia.com/gpu: 1
          env:
            - name: MODEL_NAME
              value: "bert-base-uncased"
            - name: MAX_BATCH_SIZE
              valueFrom:
                configMapKeyRef:
                  name: inference-config
                  key: max_batch_size
```

Key operations:

```bash
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
kubectl rollout status deployment/inference-api
kubectl rollout history deployment/inference-api
kubectl rollout undo deployment/inference-api --to-revision=2
kubectl scale deployment/inference-api --replicas=5
```

:::interactive{component="TerminalSimulator" config='{"preset":"kubectl-basics"}'}

## 4. Services

Services provide stable networking endpoints for dynamic sets of Pods:

| Type           | Scope              | Use Case                      |
|----------------|--------------------|-------------------------------|
| **ClusterIP**  | Cluster-internal   | Internal microservices        |
| **NodePort**   | Node IP + port     | Development / testing         |
| **LoadBalancer** | Cloud LB         | Production external access    |
| **ExternalName** | DNS CNAME        | External service aliasing     |

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: inference-api-svc
  namespace: ml-serving
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: inference-api
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 80
      targetPort: 8080
    - name: grpc
      port: 8001
      targetPort: 8001
```

DNS resolution: `inference-api-svc.ml-serving.svc.cluster.local`

## 5. ConfigMaps and Secrets

### 5.1 ConfigMaps

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: training-config
  namespace: ml-training
data:
  BATCH_SIZE: "64"
  LEARNING_RATE: "0.001"
  NUM_EPOCHS: "100"
  config.yaml: |
    model:
      name: bert-base
      hidden_size: 768
    training:
      warmup_steps: 1000
      weight_decay: 0.01
```

### 5.2 Secrets

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: registry-creds
type: kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson
data:
  .dockerconfigjson: <base64-encoded-docker-config>
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: s3-credentials
type: Opaque
stringData:
  AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: "AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE"
  AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: "wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY"
```

Reference in pods:

```yaml
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: training-config
        - secretRef:
            name: s3-credentials
      volumeMounts:
        - name: config-vol
          mountPath: /etc/config
  volumes:
    - name: config-vol
      configMap:
        name: training-config
        items:
          - key: config.yaml
            path: config.yaml
```

## 6. Namespaces and RBAC

### 6.1 Namespaces for Multi-Tenancy

```bash
kubectl create namespace ml-training
kubectl create namespace ml-serving
kubectl create namespace data-engineering
```

### 6.2 ResourceQuotas

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: ml-training-quota
  namespace: ml-training
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "128"
    requests.memory: "512Gi"
    requests.nvidia.com/gpu: "16"
    limits.cpu: "128"
    limits.memory: "512Gi"
    limits.nvidia.com/gpu: "16"
    pods: "50"
    persistentvolumeclaims: "20"
```

### 6.3 RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)

```yaml
# Role вҖ” scoped to a namespace
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: ml-developer
  namespace: ml-training
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "pods/log", "pods/exec", "services", "configmaps"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "delete"]
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update"]
  - apiGroups: ["batch"]
    resources: ["jobs", "cronjobs"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "delete"]
---
# RoleBinding вҖ” binds role to a user/group
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: ml-developer-binding
  namespace: ml-training
subjects:
  - kind: Group
    name: ml-team
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: ml-developer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
```

## 7. Persistent Volumes and StorageClasses

### 7.1 StorageClass

```yaml
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: fast-nvme
provisioner: kubernetes.io/aws-ebs
parameters:
  type: io2
  iopsPerGB: "50"
reclaimPolicy: Retain
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer
allowVolumeExpansion: true
```

### 7.2 PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC)

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: training-data-pvc
  namespace: ml-training
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteMany          # Required for multi-node access
  storageClassName: fast-nvme
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 500Gi
```

### 7.3 For HPC Shared Filesystems (NFS/Lustre)

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: lustre-shared-data
spec:
  capacity:
    storage: 10Ti
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteMany
  nfs:
    server: lustre-gw.hpc.local
    path: /lustre/shared/datasets
  persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Retain
```

## 8. Network Policies

Restrict traffic between namespaces for security:

```yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-all-ingress
  namespace: ml-training
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress: []
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-from-serving
  namespace: ml-training
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: model-registry
  ingress:
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: ml-serving
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8080
```

lessons.title: GPU Scheduling in Kubernetes
objectives: Install and configure the NVIDIA GPU Operator and device plugin
Schedule GPU workloads with resource requests
Configure Multi-Instance GPU (MIG) for hardware-level GPU sharing
Monitor GPUs with DCGM and Prometheus
Submit distributed training jobs using PyTorchJob and MPIJob CRDs
Compare GPU sharing strategies: time-slicing vs MPS vs MIG
lessons.content_md:
# GPU Scheduling in Kubernetes

## 1. NVIDIA GPU Operator and Device Plugin

The **NVIDIA GPU Operator** automates the deployment of all software
components needed to run GPU workloads on Kubernetes:

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ              NVIDIA GPU Operator                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                                   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ NVIDIA Driver в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ NVIDIA Container Toolkit в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (DaemonSet)  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (DaemonSet)              в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ Device Plugin в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ GPU Feature Discovery    в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ

в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

:::interactive{component="K8sVisualizer" config='{"preset":"ml-training"}'}

### 1.1 Installing the GPU Operator

```bash
# Add the NVIDIA Helm repository
helm repo add nvidia https://helm.ngc.nvidia.com/nvidia
helm repo update

# Install the GPU Operator
helm install gpu-operator nvidia/gpu-operator \
    --namespace gpu-operator \
    --create-namespace \
    --set driver.enabled=true \
    --set toolkit.enabled=true \
    --set devicePlugin.enabled=true \
    --set dcgmExporter.enabled=true \
    --set migManager.enabled=true \
    --set gfd.enabled=true
```

### 1.2 The Device Plugin

The **NVIDIA device plugin** runs as a DaemonSet on every GPU node. It:
- Registers `nvidia.com/gpu` as a schedulable resource
- Reports GPU count and health to the kubelet
- Allocates specific GPUs to containers via environment variables

```bash
# Verify GPU availability across the cluster
kubectl get nodes -o custom-columns=\
  NAME:.metadata.name,\
  GPUS:.status.capacity.nvidia\\.com/gpu,\
  GPU_TYPE:.metadata.labels.nvidia\\.com/gpu\\.product

# Example output:
# NAME          GPUS   GPU_TYPE
# gpu-node-01   8      NVIDIA-A100-SXM4-80GB
# gpu-node-02   8      NVIDIA-A100-SXM4-80GB
# gpu-node-03   4      NVIDIA-H100-SXM5-80GB
```

### 1.3 GPU Feature Discovery (GFD)

GFD automatically labels nodes with GPU properties:

```yaml
# Labels added by GFD
nvidia.com/gpu.product: "NVIDIA-A100-SXM4-80GB"
nvidia.com/gpu.memory: "81920"           # MB
nvidia.com/gpu.count: "8"
nvidia.com/gpu.family: "ampere"
nvidia.com/gpu.compute.major: "8"
nvidia.com/gpu.compute.minor: "0"
nvidia.com/cuda.driver.major: "535"
nvidia.com/mig.capable: "true"
```

Use these labels for targeted scheduling:

```yaml
nodeSelector:
  nvidia.com/gpu.product: "NVIDIA-A100-SXM4-80GB"
  nvidia.com/gpu.count: "8"
```

## 2. GPU Resource Scheduling

### 2.1 Requesting GPUs in Pods

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: gpu-training
spec:
  containers:
    - name: trainer
      image: nvcr.io/nvidia/pytorch:24.01-py3
      command: ["torchrun", "--nproc_per_node=4", "train.py"]
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: "32"
          memory: "128Gi"
        limits:
          cpu: "32"
          memory: "128Gi"
          nvidia.com/gpu: 4
  restartPolicy: Never
```

**Key rules for GPU scheduling:**

| Rule                        | Detail                                    |
|-----------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| Integer only                | Cannot request 0.5 GPUs (use MIG instead) |
| Limits required             | GPUs must be in `limits` (not just requests) |
| No overcommit (default)     | 4 GPU request needs node with вүҘ4 free GPUs |
| Same node only              | All GPUs for a pod come from one node     |
| requests defaults to limits | If only limits set, requests = limits     |

### 2.2 Node Affinity for GPU Types

```yaml
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        nodeSelectorTerms:
          - matchExpressions:
              - key: nvidia.com/gpu.product
                operator: In
                values:
                  - "NVIDIA-A100-SXM4-80GB"
                  - "NVIDIA-H100-SXM5-80GB"
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        - weight: 100
          preference:
            matchExpressions:
              - key: nvidia.com/gpu.product
                operator: In
                values:
                  - "NVIDIA-H100-SXM5-80GB"
```

## 3. Multi-Instance GPU (MIG)

NVIDIA A100 and H100 GPUs support **MIG** вҖ” hardware-level
partitioning into isolated GPU instances with **guaranteed**
compute and memory:

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ A100 80GB в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ                                                   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Full GPU: 108 SMs, 80 GB HBM2e                  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                                   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  MIG Partitioning Options:                        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ 7Г— 1g.10gb  (each: ~15 SMs, 10 GB)         в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ 1Г— 3g.40gb       в”Ӯ в”Ӯ 4Г— 1g.10gb           в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ (42 SMs, 40 GB) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ (each: 15 SMs, 10 GB)в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ 1Г— 7g.80gb  (full GPU, 108 SMs, 80 GB)      в”Ӯ в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

:::interactive{component="GPUMemoryVisualizer"}

### 3.1 MIG Profiles

| Profile (A100 80GB)  | SMs  | Memory  | Use Case                    |
|----------------------|------|---------|-----------------------------|
| `1g.10gb`            | ~15  | 10 GB   | Small inference, dev        |
| `2g.20gb`            | ~28  | 20 GB   | Medium inference            |
| `3g.40gb`            | ~42  | 40 GB   | Fine-tuning, medium models  |
| `4g.40gb`            | ~56  | 40 GB   | Training medium models      |
| `7g.80gb`            | 108  | 80 GB   | Large model training        |

### 3.2 Enabling MIG

```bash
# Enable MIG mode on GPU 0
sudo nvidia-smi -i 0 -mig 1

# Create MIG instances (7Г— 1g.10gb)
sudo nvidia-smi mig -i 0 -cgi 19,19,19,19,19,19,19 -C

# List MIG instances
nvidia-smi mig -i 0 -lgi
nvidia-smi mig -i 0 -lci

# Destroy MIG instances
sudo nvidia-smi mig -i 0 -dci
sudo nvidia-smi mig -i 0 -dgi
```

### 3.3 MIG in Kubernetes

The device plugin exposes each MIG slice as a separate resource:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: inference-small
spec:
  containers:
    - name: model
      image: myregistry/bert-serve:v1.0
      resources:
        limits:
          nvidia.com/mig-1g.10gb: 1   # Request one MIG slice
```

### 3.4 MIG Manager ConfigMap

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: mig-parted-config
  namespace: gpu-operator
data:
  config.yaml: |
    version: v1
    mig-configs:
      all-1g.10gb:
        - device-filter: ["0x20B210DE", "0x20B510DE"]
          devices: all
          mig-enabled: true
          mig-devices:
            "1g.10gb": 7
      mixed-training-inference:
        - device-filter: ["0x20B210DE"]
          devices: [0,1,2,3]
          mig-enabled: true
          mig-devices:
            "3g.40gb": 1
            "1g.10gb": 4
        - device-filter: ["0x20B210DE"]
          devices: [4,5,6,7]
          mig-enabled: false
```

:::interactive{component="CUDAVisualizer" config='{"preset":"occupancy"}'}

## 4. GPU Monitoring with DCGM

The **NVIDIA Data Center GPU Manager (DCGM)** provides deep GPU
telemetry. The DCGM Exporter runs as a DaemonSet and exposes
Prometheus metrics:

```bash
# Key DCGM metrics exposed to Prometheus
DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL          # GPU utilization %
DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED           # Framebuffer memory used (MB)
DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_FREE           # Framebuffer memory free (MB)
DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_TEMP          # GPU temperature (В°C)
DCGM_FI_DEV_POWER_USAGE       # Power consumption (W)
DCGM_FI_DEV_SM_CLOCK          # SM clock frequency (MHz)
DCGM_FI_DEV_PCIE_TX_THROUGHPUT  # PCIe TX bandwidth
DCGM_FI_DEV_PCIE_RX_THROUGHPUT  # PCIe RX bandwidth
DCGM_FI_DEV_NVLINK_BANDWIDTH_TOTAL  # NVLink bandwidth
DCGM_FI_DEV_XID_ERRORS        # GPU XID error count
```

### 4.1 DCGM Exporter Configuration

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: dcgm-exporter-config
  namespace: gpu-operator
data:
  dcgm-metrics.csv: |
    DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL, gauge, GPU utilization
    DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED, gauge, Frame buffer used (MB)
    DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_FREE, gauge, Frame buffer free (MB)
    DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_TEMP, gauge, GPU temperature (C)
    DCGM_FI_DEV_POWER_USAGE, gauge, Power usage (W)
    DCGM_FI_DEV_PCIE_TX_THROUGHPUT, counter, PCIe TX (KB/s)
    DCGM_FI_DEV_PCIE_RX_THROUGHPUT, counter, PCIe RX (KB/s)
```

### 4.2 Grafana Dashboard Queries

```promql
# Average GPU utilization per node
avg by (node) (DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL{namespace="gpu-operator"})

# GPU memory usage per pod
DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED{namespace="ml-training"} /
(DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED{namespace="ml-training"} + DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_FREE{namespace="ml-training"}) * 100

# GPU temperature alert (> 85В°C)
DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_TEMP > 85

# Total cluster GPU utilization
avg(DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL)
```

## 5. Training Jobs with CRDs

### 5.1 Kubeflow PyTorchJob

```yaml
apiVersion: kubeflow.org/v1
kind: PyTorchJob
metadata:
  name: llm-finetune
  namespace: ml-training
spec:
  elasticPolicy:
    minReplicas: 2
    maxReplicas: 8
    rdzvBackend: c10d
  pytorchReplicaSpecs:
    Master:
      replicas: 1
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: pytorch
              image: myregistry/llm-train:v2.0
              command:
                - torchrun
                - --nproc_per_node=8
                - --nnodes=$(WORLD_SIZE)
                - train.py
                - --bf16
                - --gradient-checkpointing
              env:
                - name: NCCL_DEBUG
                  value: "INFO"
                - name: NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME
                  value: "eth0"
              resources:
                limits:
                  cpu: "64"
                  memory: "512Gi"
                  nvidia.com/gpu: 8
              volumeMounts:
                - name: datasets
                  mountPath: /data
                  readOnly: true
                - name: checkpoints
                  mountPath: /checkpoints
          volumes:
            - name: datasets
              persistentVolumeClaim:
                claimName: training-data
            - name: checkpoints
              persistentVolumeClaim:
                claimName: checkpoint-store
    Worker:
      replicas: 3
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: pytorch
              image: myregistry/llm-train:v2.0
              command:
                - torchrun
                - --nproc_per_node=8
                - --nnodes=$(WORLD_SIZE)
                - train.py
                - --bf16
                - --gradient-checkpointing
              env:
                - name: NCCL_DEBUG
                  value: "INFO"
              resources:
                limits:
                  cpu: "64"
                  memory: "512Gi"
                  nvidia.com/gpu: 8
```

### 5.2 Kubeflow MPIJob

```yaml
apiVersion: kubeflow.org/v2beta1
kind: MPIJob
metadata:
  name: horovod-training
  namespace: ml-training
spec:
  slotsPerWorker: 8
  mpiReplicaSpecs:
    Launcher:
      replicas: 1
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: launcher
              image: myregistry/horovod-train:v1.0
              command:
                - mpirun
                - --allow-run-as-root
                - -np
                - "32"
                - --bind-to
                - none
                - -x
                - NCCL_DEBUG=INFO
                - python
                - train.py
    Worker:
      replicas: 4
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: worker
              image: myregistry/horovod-train:v1.0
              resources:
                limits:
                  nvidia.com/gpu: 8
```

## 6. GPU Sharing Strategies

| Strategy        | Isolation  | Memory Isolation | Overhead | Supported GPUs  |
|-----------------|-----------|-----------------|----------|-----------------|
| **Time-slicing**| Software  | None            | Low      | All NVIDIA GPUs |
| **MPS**         | Software  | Partial (limits)| Very low | Volta+          |
| **MIG**         | Hardware  | Full            | None     | A100, H100 only |

### 6.1 Time-Slicing Configuration

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: device-plugin-config
  namespace: gpu-operator
data:
  config.yaml: |
    version: v1
    sharing:
      timeSlicing:
        renameByDefault: false
        failRequestsGreaterThanOne: false
        resources:
          - name: nvidia.com/gpu
            replicas: 4
```

### 6.2 MPS (Multi-Process Service)

```yaml
# Enable MPS via GPU Operator
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: device-plugin-config
  namespace: gpu-operator
data:
  config.yaml: |
    version: v1
    sharing:
      mps:
        renameByDefault: false
        failRequestsGreaterThanOne: false
        resources:
          - name: nvidia.com/gpu
            replicas: 10
            devices: all
```

Choose based on workload:
- **Time-slicing**: Development, interactive notebooks
- **MPS**: Inference with many small models, low-latency
- **MIG**: Production inference with SLA requirements

exercises.title: MIG Configuration for Mixed Workloads

Write a MIG Manager ConfigMap that configures an A100 80GB node with a
mixed partitioning strategy: GPUs 0-3 each get 1Г— 3g.40gb and 4Г— 1g.10gb
MIG slices (for a mix of training and inference), and GPUs 4-7 remain
as full GPUs (MIG disabled) for large training jobs. Use the A100 device
filter 0x20B210DE.

apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: mig-parted-config

  config.yaml: |
    version: v1
    mig-configs:
      # TODO: Define 'mixed-workload' config

      #     - 4Г— 1g.10gb slices (for inference)

```yaml
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
```
